turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument this morning in Case 12簫9012, Robers v. United States. Mr. Green.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: With respect to property crimes, Section 3663A of the Mandatory Victims Restitution Act requires an offset against a restitution order when any part of the property is returned to the victim. This case concerns secured loans for the purchase of real estate. Pursuant to those loans, the lenders took a property interest in the subject real estate, namely, the right to foreclose and take title in the event of default. When the lenders foreclosed and took title, that represented a fully realized return of their preexisting property right. Section 3663A further requires that that property be valued as of the date of its return and not at some later date when the lender sells to a third party.

Now, the victim would get to keep the extra amount of the money in the same way that happens in any foreclosure proceeding when there's no fraud involved. If a bank forecloses 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: You would 簫簫 you would 簫簫 you would agree that a different rule applies if there is some unreasonable delay in disposing of the property?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes. And in our view, that's a proximate cause question, because then the victim's net losses wouldn't be caused by the defendant's fraud.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: And what do you do in the case of some dramatic development between the return of the house as opposed to the 簫簫 the property and its 簫簫 and its sale?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: It depends what sort of dramatic development you mean.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, you know, something is discovered with respect to the property that nobody knew about. It's 簫簫 suddenly, it's a hazardous chemical zone and the property is 簫簫 is worthless. While when it was returned 簫簫 not returned; I don't mean to prejudge the case.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: The bank got it.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: When the bank got it, it was worth $200,000, but all of a sudden it's, you know, Love Canal or something, and it's worth nothing.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Well, it's not our view 簫簫 you know, the house is only worth what someone is willing to pay for it in an arm's簫length transaction. And so if there's something about the house that you just didn't know when the bank got the house, then that really wouldn't be the value of the house. The value of the house is what you can sell it for, and if there's something that's 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, no. But my point is, is it the value you could sell it for before this discovery of an extraordinary condition, or is it the value afterward?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: I think in that case, it would probably be whatever the money that the bank can get for the house. But if there's some other type of 簫簫 of event, like if 簫簫 you know, if there's an earthquake in the middle of the country that destroys the house, then I think that would be 簫簫 that would be sort of a classic example of something that would break the chain of proximate cause. And so what would happen in that case usually is the bank would get paid by its insurance company, compensated for the loss of the house, and you would sort of value the house at the time that it was 簫簫 it was destroyed after the bank acquired it. And then the insurance company in that case wouldn't be entitled to any restitution from the defendant, because it wouldn't be the defendant's actions that caused the destruction of the house. What he's saying is that just the fact that the 簫簫 that the housing market went down is an intervening nonforeseeable event that breaks the chain of causation. Under our view, that's absolutely not correct. The very nature of the defendant's scheme injected the health of the housing market into the ultimate determination of the victim's net losses. He 簫簫 his design was that he would take money from the banks and promise to pay it back. He had no intention to pay it back, in fact never made a single payment. That necessarily and foreseeably triggered the foreclosure, which caused the banks to own the houses and then caused them to put them on the market and sell. It's absolutely foreseeable that it takes banks a certain amount of time to sell the houses, absolutely foreseeable that the value of the houses will fluctuate over time. No one has to be able to predict what the houses would have been worth on a particular date in the future to be able to say that it was foreseeable that the value would change. And the MVRA allocates any risk from a change in property to a defendant, not to the victim. And that's consistent with our view. If there are no further questions, I can rest 簫簫 I guess I would just like to end by reiterating our main point, which is that the text of the statute is very clear in this case, and what it focuses on is how much of the property that was lost has been returned to the victim before sentencing. When the property that was lost was a car, you don't get an offset for returning pineapples. When the property that was lost was money, you don't get an offset for returning the house. But in this case, as in almost all mortgage fraud cases, the banks were able to get back some of the property they lost when they sold the houses, and so there's no valuation question here. It's not a question of how much of the property that was lost did the banks get back. The district court knew 100 percent for sure because the houses were sold before Mr. Robers was even charged with a crime.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, what if they get 簫簫 they get the house and as soon as they get it, there are ten offers at $500,000 or more, and the amount of the restitution is, you know, $300,000 or whatever. But the bank says, you know, I think the market is going to go even higher, so I'm not going to sell it right away, I'm going to wait, not an unreasonable length of time, but I'm going to wait. And then the market craters. I mean, is the same rule then, that they only get what they were able to sell it for?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: I mean, as long as what the bank 簫簫 what the bank did was commercially reasonable. If the bank 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I know banks like to get rid of property as soon as possible, but, you know, they make decisions at some point, let's wait before we sell this. Is that commercially reasonable?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: I mean, it's hard to know. You know, you wouldn't want to 簫簫 we wouldn't want to judge that with 100 percent perfect hindsight, right? You'd want to say 簫簫 if you were in the bank's shoes at the time it made the decision, was that a commercially reasonable decision, and you know, that's a decision that would have to be made in those kind of cases.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, why should the defendant here subsidize the banks playing the market? That's what you are saying.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: No, what I'm saying is 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The bank is playing the market. It says, yeah, we could sell it for so much now, but, yeah, it may go up. And you're saying that, you know, he's paying for the bank's roll of the dice.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: So if there really 簫簫 if there really is evidence that the bank is sort of really rolling the dice, then that might be a harder question. Generally, what the bank wants is to turn the house into money because the bank is not in the business of owning houses. It's in the business of having money. And again, the banks don't have in their mind that they're going to get a restitution payment when they take the houses. What they have in mind is that they're going to take a loss. If they've had to foreclose on a house, it's a bad mortgage, generally they are going to get back less for the house than what they got for the mortgage, and if that happens they consider it a loss, and I think any business person would consider that a loss. And it's perfectly foreseeable that it's going to take the bank a certain amount of time to turn the house into cash. But when that's happened before sentencing, it's really an easy case to apply Section 3663A because you absolutely know for sure how much of the lost property has been returned to the victim and it's the amount of the money they got for the sale, or for the property.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But if I could just be clear about what you're saying, you're saying it's not the sale price when the sale is for a nominal amount; is that right?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And it's not the sale price if the lender has done something commercially unreasonable?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And so if that's right and both of those things sound right, but why doesn't that kind of explode your statutory argument, because then it's not any part of the property that's returned.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Because the other parts of the statute require 簫簫 have a proximate cause requirement, and so you are only 簫簫 the victim is only entitled to get restitution for the amount of its losses that are attributable to the defendant's criminal conduct. And so if part of the bank's losses, i.e., the smaller amount of the return it gets for selling the houses, is because of the bank's independent action, then that part of the loss is not attributable to the defendant.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I followed you up through here, I think. You're into B.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: He is pointing to little (ii) and you're saying that really is not relevant here because it's cash. None of the property was returned; they gave him a house, not the cash.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Well, we're saying it's relevant when the bank sells the house because then part of the property is returned.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, right. What they have is the house.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: They have the house until they sell it and then they have cash.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Oh, I see.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: And so it's the cash they get when they sell the house 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And that is if they've sold it before the sentencing.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Right. Which happens in most every case. Not every case, but 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, let's suppose it doesn't and they still have it as of the time of sentencing, then we're over into 3(a). It's an 簫簫 3664.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: It's an in簫kind payment. The judge has discretion as to how to value it. He could say, sell it within 90 days, and we will take that.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Or he could say if you don't want to do that, we call in the assessors.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: It wouldn't be considered an offset under Section 3663A(b)(1)(B)(ii).

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: You're saying because (3)(ii) applies, that when it's transformed into cash prior to the sentencing, then they have received 簫簫

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Some part of the property.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 some part of the money back 簫簫

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 and often that will work in favor of the defendant.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: It will because 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Sometimes, as in this case, it won't if the housing market collapses.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Sometimes it goes down, but other times it goes up. And the trend over time is that the housing market goes up, and so more often than not in the larger picture, it will inure to the benefit of the defendant.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So where do you value for the person who 簫簫 the 簫簫 the creditor, the secured creditor who acts unreasonably? Let's assume an unreasonable act, and you said it might have been commercially unreasonable to hold on to the property after somebody offered them twice the amount of their mortgage.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Well, I think in those unusual circumstances, it would be as you suggested earlier, which is that you would value it as of the date it would have been reasonable to dispose of the property in a fair market, arm's簫length transaction.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So 簫簫

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: 簫簫 and that's usually not 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 what do you think of this test? In calculating a creditor's loss resulting from a secure transaction, the restitution order 簫簫 award should be offset by the amount that a reasonably diligent creditor, under the same or similar circumstances, could or would 簫簫 or did 簫簫 could, would or did obtain for the collateral?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: I think generally that's fine. I think in the 簫簫 really, in the mine run of cases, the lion's share of cases, you are going to be sort of overly complicating the district court's inquiry, because in almost all of the cases, the houses have been sold, and so you know you can look at the dollar figure that the banks got, and that's the amount you can use. And it 簫簫 and that fits into what you described, but just sort of describes it in a more complicated way, I think, in more 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, it's not just more complicated. It's a question of, I guess, who has the burden. As I understood, what you are proposing is you get the sale price, but it's 簫簫 it's available to the defendant to show that the sale price was unreasonably low because of gamesmanship on the part of the bank or some other reason, right?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: I think that would be available to the defendant.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: I think 簫簫

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You would prefer that to the other formulation, wouldn't you?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Yes, and I think there is a presumption that banks are going to act in a commercially reasonable way because they have no expectation of getting any money back through the restitution process.

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: If the sale 簫簫 if at the time of sentencing, the house has not yet been sold, but there's no 簫簫 there's good reason for the delay, because for example, there are no bids at an earlier time, could the judge defer the restitution order until the house is sold?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: So as Justice Breyer suggested, under our view, the court 簫簫 the district court's first resort would be to say 簫簫 to push off the determination of the restitution amount for 90 days under 3664(d)(5), and give the 簫簫 give the bank a little more time to sell the house, because in that case, a determination 簫簫 you wouldn't be able to ascertain the net losses of the victim. If 簫簫 if a bank still couldn't sell the house, then I think under this Court's decision in Dolan, what the court could do is say: Yes, I'm going to order restitution; the restitution will be for the full amount of the mortgages, the money that was lost minus whatever amount of money the banks get back when they sell the houses. And if the bank has not been able to sell the house at that point, it's really to everyone's benefit to give them the chance to do it, because if no one wants to buy the house and you force the district court to value the house at that point, it's going to give it a really low value and that doesn't help the defendant, it doesn't really help the bank 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What does the defendant do if they act unreasonably after that order?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: Then you 簫簫

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If the bank does a nominal sale and only gets $100?

turns.name: HARRINGTON
turns.text: So then I think the district court would have retained jurisdiction to fill in an amount related, blank, for the amount of money the bank got back, and there could be proceedings about what that amount should be.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Counsel. Mr. Green, you have 5 minutes remaining.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: It's important, Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kennedy, to understand that defendants have no compulsory process in this restitution簫ordering scheme. So that if there is a delay, Justice Ginsburg, in the ultimate sale of the property or maybe it's for a nominal amount or maybe it's for a discounted amount, the defendant has no way to try and look behind that transaction. They can't issue a subpoena. They can't 簫簫 they could call a witness, but it would only be if it was a friendly witness. And here, Benjamin Robers sits with a $219,000 restitution order for which he is jointly and severally liable, as my colleague said 182 years it takes to pay off, and there is no way for his attorney, us, to get behind the transactions at issue and challenge them.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why not? Why not just say, hey, I'll tell you what happened here. They 簫簫 we gave him a house worth 100,000, and they sold it for a dollar, and they only want to subtract a dollar, the Government. But I will tell you something. I would count that house as an in簫kind payment 簫簫

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: It's 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 簫簫 an in簫kind payment, and they were wrong to sell it. And it was worth 100,000, that was totally crooked, their selling it. And therefore, I want you to say, fine, perfectly right subtract $1 under (ii) 簫簫 (ii), but subtract 99,000 more because of the in簫kind payment that was made. Would you say that?

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: No. And a 簫簫 and a 簫簫

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: Because, Your Honor, in an obvious case like that, the defendant would have the ability to 簫簫 to point to the facts and circumstances and say, you know, that 簫簫 that, it doesn't appear, to be an arm's簫length transaction, Your Honor, and that would be obvious evidence. But much more sophisticated things are going on here, Justice Breyer. This is a situation where if we take a look at the Inlet Shore Drive property, it sells for $166,000, which appears to be quite a bit lower than its value when the bank got ahold of that property for nothing 簫簫 for no reason that Mr. Robers had control over. If that property had been burning down, Justice Alito, he would not have been able to go in and throw a bucket of water on it because that would have been trespassing. He had no dominion or control over that property. So in a situation where 簫簫

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but the only reason he would have been trespassing is because, in fact, he did not live in the house as he promised the bank he would when the bank gave him the money.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So why shouldn't he bear the responsibility for that consequence?

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: Because of the bank's dominion, because of the situations we've been talking about here where the bank holds on to the property unreasonably too long, right into the teeth of a great recession 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: There was no 簫簫 there was no 簫簫

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: 簫簫 that he's not 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: 簫簫 evidence of that in this case, that the bank held on too long.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: Well, it was 33 months after foreclosure. The foreclosure on Inlet Shore Drive took place on 簫簫 in February 2006. The sale 簫簫

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: The answer that Ms. Harrington gave was that there were no bids.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor?

turns.name: RUTH GINSBURG
turns.text: I think she said there were no bids.

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: There were no bids at the 簫簫 at the foreclosure sale, but that frequently happens that there's no bids at the foreclosure sale, Your Honor. We don't know 簫簫 and this is another point about the defendant's discovery. We don't know what happens after 簫簫 after the 簫簫 after the lender takes control of the property. We don't know whether it's taking unreasonable risks or not unreasonable risks. And, again, the defendant doesn't have the capacity. The other point 簫簫

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Is there any reason why the 簫簫 why the sentencing judge, where there's an 簫簫 an allegation and maybe a 簫簫 a suggestion that there was unreasonable delay or something unreasonable was going on require the Government to provide some substantiation for 簫簫 for what happened?

turns.name: GREEN
turns.text: There 簫簫 there could be, Your Honor. The 簫簫 the judge 簫簫 yeah, the defense could ask the judge, Your Honor, please, could we get some discovery here? And it would be completely up to the court whether the court would want to do that or not. But the point is that it's opaque to the defendant. And unless the defendant can come up with something obvious, the defendant would have absolutely no opportunity to produce evidence. The other point I want to make here is a fundamental one. And respectfully, I think we're 簫簫 we're 簫簫 in talking about baseball bats and pineapples and tomatoes, we're getting lost about what's actually at issue here. This is a secured transaction. This is something that the 簫簫 that the victim lender agreed to take back in the event of a default. They took a property interest. That's a property interest. This Court said that in Pasquantino. That's a property interest that they took in that particular piece of property. Benjamin Robers did not take $330,000 and say, I'm going to go buy the best house I can with that. The lender said, Benjamin Robers, here is $330,000 in your name so that you can buy this particular piece of property, subject to our interest. And so when the default took place, there needed to be a 簫簫 there needed to be a return of that property interest, and it happened.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Counsel. The case is submitted.
